APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses multilingual semantic parsing with a principled UD-to-logical-form interface and cross-lingual evaluation on WebQuestions/GraphQuestions, which squarely fits a top NLP/ML conference audience. The methodology and comparisons to strong baselines (including achieving best reported GraphQuestions English result) make it relevant and of interest to the community. However, reproducibility details appear incomplete in the summary (no mention of code availability or hyperparameter/reporting), which are important for top-tier acceptance and hence prevent a top score. Overall it is a good fit with minor concerns about experimental reproducibility and reporting. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it proposes a broadly applicable multilingual semantic interface (UDEPLAMBDA) that maps UD to logical forms and extends prior work (DEPLAMBDA) with graph support and BIND, addressing core problems in cross-lingual semantic parsing. The methodology and evaluation meet venue standards—using English, German, and Spanish evaluations on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions (including new translations) and comparisons to strong baselines—while limitations and related work are discussed, making it highly relevant to the conference audience focused on language-independent meaning representations and semantic parsing.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses multilingual semantic parsing with a principled UD-to-logical-form interface and cross-lingual evaluation on WebQuestions/GraphQuestions, which squarely fits a top NLP/ML conference audience. The methodology and comparisons to strong baselines (including achieving best reported GraphQuestions English result) make it relevant and of interest to the community. However, reproducibility details appear incomplete in the summary (no mention of code availability or hyperparameter/reporting), which are important for top-tier acceptance and hence prevent a top score. Overall it is a good fit with minor concerns about experimental reproducibility and reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it proposes a broadly applicable multilingual semantic interface (UDEPLAMBDA) that maps UD to logical forms and extends prior work (DEPLAMBDA) with graph support and BIND, addressing core problems in cross-lingual semantic parsing. The methodology and evaluation meet venue standards—using English, German, and Spanish evaluations on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions (including new translations) and comparisons to strong baselines—while limitations and related work are discussed, making it highly relevant to the conference audience focused on language-independent meaning representations and semantic parsing.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important practical problem—building a (nearly) language-independent semantic interface from Universal Dependencies—which matters for cross-lingual semantic parsing and KBQA. Demonstrating UDEPLAMBDA across English, German, and Spanish and achieving state-of-the-art on GraphQuestions suggests the approach materially affects how practitioners can leverage UD for meaning representation. While the restriction to UD and omission of lexicalized phenomena (quantifiers, negation, lexical disambiguation) limit immediate applicability, the graph-oriented BIND mechanism and multi-language dataset contributions open useful avenues for follow-up work and integration into multilingual pipelines. | B(4): This paper addresses an important problem—providing a language-independent semantic interface for UD—and demonstrates practical cross-lingual gains (English, German, Spanish) on Freebase QA, showing UD can yield compositional logical forms and handle graph-structured input via a novel BIND mechanism. By releasing German and Spanish translations of benchmark QA datasets and outperforming strong baselines (best reported on GraphQuestions English), the work is likely to shift how researchers view UD as a substrate for semantic parsing and to spur follow-up work on UD-based meaning representations. Notwithstanding acknowledged limits (no lexicalized disambiguation, quantifier/negation/intentional verbs not handled), the approach opens significant research directions for improving language coverage and integrating lexical semantics, and is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem—building a (nearly) language-independent semantic interface from Universal Dependencies—which matters for cross-lingual semantic parsing and KBQA. Demonstrating UDEPLAMBDA across English, German, and Spanish and achieving state-of-the-art on GraphQuestions suggests the approach materially affects how practitioners can leverage UD for meaning representation. While the restriction to UD and omission of lexicalized phenomena (quantifiers, negation, lexical disambiguation) limit immediate applicability, the graph-oriented BIND mechanism and multi-language dataset contributions open useful avenues for follow-up work and integration into multilingual pipelines.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important problem—providing a language-independent semantic interface for UD—and demonstrates practical cross-lingual gains (English, German, Spanish) on Freebase QA, showing UD can yield compositional logical forms and handle graph-structured input via a novel BIND mechanism. By releasing German and Spanish translations of benchmark QA datasets and outperforming strong baselines (best reported on GraphQuestions English), the work is likely to shift how researchers view UD as a substrate for semantic parsing and to spur follow-up work on UD-based meaning representations. Notwithstanding acknowledged limits (no lexicalized disambiguation, quantifier/negation/intentional verbs not handled), the approach opens significant research directions for improving language coverage and integrating lexical semantics, and is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents substantial cross-lingual experiments (English, German, Spanish) on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions and compares to multiple strong baselines, which supports the main claim that UDEPLAMBDA generalizes across UD. However, the summary indicates no reported hyperparameters or code release, limited detail on evaluation metrics, and no clear ablation or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., enhancement heuristics, BIND mechanism), which weakens reproducibility and causal attribution. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and the authors explicitly discuss important limitations (lexicalized phenomena, quantifiers, negation), which is good. Overall the experimental evidence is adequate but missing critical reproducibility and ablation details needed for a stronger verdict. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong in cross-lingual scope and datasets (English, German, Spanish; WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) and shows broad comparisons to multiple baselines, supporting the main claims. However, the summary indicates no ablation or sensitivity analyses of key design choices (e.g., the BIND mechanism, enhancement steps), and evaluation metrics/hyperparameters and code release are not specified, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations are explicitly discussed (lexicalized phenomena, quantifiers, negation). Overall solid empirical work with minor but important gaps in analysis and reproducibility detail.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents substantial cross-lingual experiments (English, German, Spanish) on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions and compares to multiple strong baselines, which supports the main claim that UDEPLAMBDA generalizes across UD. However, the summary indicates no reported hyperparameters or code release, limited detail on evaluation metrics, and no clear ablation or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., enhancement heuristics, BIND mechanism), which weakens reproducibility and causal attribution. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and the authors explicitly discuss important limitations (lexicalized phenomena, quantifiers, negation), which is good. Overall the experimental evidence is adequate but missing critical reproducibility and ablation details needed for a stronger verdict.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in cross-lingual scope and datasets (English, German, Spanish; WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) and shows broad comparisons to multiple baselines, supporting the main claims. However, the summary indicates no ablation or sensitivity analyses of key design choices (e.g., the BIND mechanism, enhancement steps), and evaluation metrics/hyperparameters and code release are not specified, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations are explicitly discussed (lexicalized phenomena, quantifiers, negation). Overall solid empirical work with minor but important gaps in analysis and reproducibility detail.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, multilingual evaluation and translated datasets) are stated up front, making the flow from method to experiments easy to follow. Notation around lambda-calculus and UD operations appears consistent and related work is thoroughly covered; examples and results (e.g., best English GraphQuestions) are presented to support claims. However, presentation leaves out some reproducibility details that affect clarity: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and implementation details (code availability, hyperparameters) are not reported, which creates ambiguity about experimental comparability. Overall clear and readable, but would benefit from explicitly reporting metrics and run/config details. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, multilingual evaluation and translations) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. The notation around lambda-calculus and the variable-binding extension is generally consistent and well defined, and examples and evaluation summaries (including cross-lingual results and dataset translations) help make the approach concrete. A few presentation gaps remain—e.g., omission of evaluation metrics in the summary and lack of explicit mention of released code or hyperparameters—which make some experimental details harder to assess, but the writing quality is good and the exposition is clear for a reader in semantics/NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, multilingual evaluation and translated datasets) are stated up front, making the flow from method to experiments easy to follow. Notation around lambda-calculus and UD operations appears consistent and related work is thoroughly covered; examples and results (e.g., best English GraphQuestions) are presented to support claims. However, presentation leaves out some reproducibility details that affect clarity: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and implementation details (code availability, hyperparameters) are not reported, which creates ambiguity about experimental comparability. Overall clear and readable, but would benefit from explicitly reporting metrics and run/config details.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (UDEPLAMBDA, BIND mechanism, multilingual evaluation and translations) are stated early and carried through method and experiments. The notation around lambda-calculus and the variable-binding extension is generally consistent and well defined, and examples and evaluation summaries (including cross-lingual results and dataset translations) help make the approach concrete. A few presentation gaps remain—e.g., omission of evaluation metrics in the summary and lack of explicit mention of released code or hyperparameters—which make some experimental details harder to assess, but the writing quality is good and the exposition is clear for a reader in semantics/NLP.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares UDEPLAMBDA to a strong suite of prior systems (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants and other prior work) across multiple datasets and languages, and even reports best-known results on GraphQuestions for English. However, the summary lacks key experimental-detail transparency (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned), so I cannot fully verify fairness of setups and exact comparability. The authors do acknowledge limitations and discuss related approaches, but the missing reporting of evaluation conditions prevents a fully comprehensive endorsement of fairness and reproducibility. | B(4): The paper compares UDEPLAMBDA against multiple strong and relevant baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants) and reports cross-lingual evaluation on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions, even achieving the best reported English result on GraphQuestions. Comparisons appear comprehensive and the paper discusses limitations and linguistic phenomena it does not handle, indicating awareness of where prior methods might be preferable. However, crucial details that affect fairness and reproducibility (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned) are missing from the summary, leaving some uncertainty about exact parity of experimental conditions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares UDEPLAMBDA to a strong suite of prior systems (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants and other prior work) across multiple datasets and languages, and even reports best-known results on GraphQuestions for English. However, the summary lacks key experimental-detail transparency (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned), so I cannot fully verify fairness of setups and exact comparability. The authors do acknowledge limitations and discuss related approaches, but the missing reporting of evaluation conditions prevents a fully comprehensive endorsement of fairness and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares UDEPLAMBDA against multiple strong and relevant baselines (SINGLEEVENT, DEPTREE, CCGGRAPH, three GRAPHPARSER variants) and reports cross-lingual evaluation on WebQuestions and GraphQuestions, even achieving the best reported English result on GraphQuestions. Comparisons appear comprehensive and the paper discusses limitations and linguistic phenomena it does not handle, indicating awareness of where prior methods might be preferable. However, crucial details that affect fairness and reproducibility (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned) are missing from the summary, leaving some uncertainty about exact parity of experimental conditions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states and empirically supports its main claim that UDEPLAMBDA maps UD to logical forms in a largely language-independent way and shows cross-lingual gains (English, German, Spanish) against multiple baselines, including best reported result on GraphQuestions. The theoretical mechanism (binarization/substitution/composition on UD, enhancement, BIND) is coherent, though no formal proofs are required here and none appear necessary. My primary concerns are methodological: the summary omits evaluation metrics, statistical significance tests, hyperparameter reporting, and code release, and the enhancement heuristics and loss of lexical specificity introduce assumptions that are not fully quantified. These are minor given the multilingual experiments and comparative baselines, so conclusions are plausible but would be stronger with more reproducibility details and sensitivity analyses. | B(4): The paper's core technical claims—mapping UD to logical forms in a largely language-independent way and extending DEPLAMBDA to graph-structured UD via a BIND variable mechanism—are described and supported by cross-lingual QA experiments against reasonable baselines (including several graph-based parsers) and new translations for two languages. The use of UD-only information and the explicit discussion of constructions not handled (quantifiers, negation, lexical disambiguation) shows appropriate caution; however, methodological details are incomplete in the summary (evaluation metrics not stated, no mention of statistical testing or released code/hyperparameters), which weakens reproducibility and makes it harder to fully assess claimed gains. The formal treatment (lambda substitutions and BIND) appears coherent for the intended purpose and there are no obvious logical flaws in the summary, so I judge the work technically sound with minor issues around experimental reporting and scope of linguistic coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states and empirically supports its main claim that UDEPLAMBDA maps UD to logical forms in a largely language-independent way and shows cross-lingual gains (English, German, Spanish) against multiple baselines, including best reported result on GraphQuestions. The theoretical mechanism (binarization/substitution/composition on UD, enhancement, BIND) is coherent, though no formal proofs are required here and none appear necessary. My primary concerns are methodological: the summary omits evaluation metrics, statistical significance tests, hyperparameter reporting, and code release, and the enhancement heuristics and loss of lexical specificity introduce assumptions that are not fully quantified. These are minor given the multilingual experiments and comparative baselines, so conclusions are plausible but would be stronger with more reproducibility details and sensitivity analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's core technical claims—mapping UD to logical forms in a largely language-independent way and extending DEPLAMBDA to graph-structured UD via a BIND variable mechanism—are described and supported by cross-lingual QA experiments against reasonable baselines (including several graph-based parsers) and new translations for two languages. The use of UD-only information and the explicit discussion of constructions not handled (quantifiers, negation, lexical disambiguation) shows appropriate caution; however, methodological details are incomplete in the summary (evaluation metrics not stated, no mention of statistical testing or released code/hyperparameters), which weakens reproducibility and makes it harder to fully assess claimed gains. The formal treatment (lambda substitutions and BIND) appears coherent for the intended purpose and there are no obvious logical flaws in the summary, so I judge the work technically sound with minor issues around experimental reporting and scope of linguistic coverage.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and non-trivial extension of prior DEPLAMBDA work by adapting the approach to Universal Dependencies and by introducing a variable-binding (BIND) mechanism to handle graph-structured dependency input — a concrete technical innovation beyond straightforward reuse. The focus on making the mapping (almost) language-independent and the cross-lingual evaluation (including German/Spanish translations of WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) further distinguish it from earlier, mainly English-centric systems. While it sacrifices lexicalized disambiguation and omits several semantic phenomena, the methodological changes and multilingual evaluation would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts familiar with previous DEPLAMBDA and dependency-based semantic interfaces. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and concrete extension of DEPLAMBDA to Universal Dependencies that goes beyond prior UD-based semantic interfaces by handling graph-structured dependencies via a novel BIND variable-binding mechanism and by restricting composition to UD-encoded information for language independence. The cross-lingual evaluation (English, German, Spanish) and provision of translated benchmarks strengthen the claim of multilingual applicability rather than mere reapplication of known methods. While the work sacrifices lexicalized disambiguation and leaves quantifier/negation handling for future work, the methodological changes (enhancement step, BIND, UD-focused substitution/composition) are a non-trivial and clearly differentiated advance that would inform experts working on language-independent semantic interfaces.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and non-trivial extension of prior DEPLAMBDA work by adapting the approach to Universal Dependencies and by introducing a variable-binding (BIND) mechanism to handle graph-structured dependency input — a concrete technical innovation beyond straightforward reuse. The focus on making the mapping (almost) language-independent and the cross-lingual evaluation (including German/Spanish translations of WebQuestions and GraphQuestions) further distinguish it from earlier, mainly English-centric systems. While it sacrifices lexicalized disambiguation and omits several semantic phenomena, the methodological changes and multilingual evaluation would be informative and somewhat surprising to experts familiar with previous DEPLAMBDA and dependency-based semantic interfaces.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and concrete extension of DEPLAMBDA to Universal Dependencies that goes beyond prior UD-based semantic interfaces by handling graph-structured dependencies via a novel BIND variable-binding mechanism and by restricting composition to UD-encoded information for language independence. The cross-lingual evaluation (English, German, Spanish) and provision of translated benchmarks strengthen the claim of multilingual applicability rather than mere reapplication of known methods. While the work sacrifices lexicalized disambiguation and leaves quantifier/negation handling for future work, the methodological changes (enhancement step, BIND, UD-focused substitution/composition) are a non-trivial and clearly differentiated advance that would inform experts working on language-independent semantic interfaces.

main_claims: Introduce UDEPLAMBDA, a semantic interface that maps Universal Dependencies (UD) to logical forms in an (almost) language-independent way.
Extend DEPLAMBDA to operate on UD, including support for graph-structured dependency input via a variable-binding mechanism (BIND).

Perform cross-lingual evaluation (English, German, Spanish) on question answering against Freebase and report that UDEPLAMBDA outperforms strong baselines across languages and datasets, achieving the best reported result on GraphQuestions for English.
Provide German and Spanish translations of WebQuestions and GraphQuestions to facilitate multilingual evaluation.
_summary.methodology: UDEPLAMBDA restricts binarization, substitution, and composition to information encoded in the UD representation (dependency labels and POS), applies an enhancement step to add missing syntactic information and long-distance dependencies, and uses a variable-binding mechanism (BIND) and lambda-calculus substitutions to derive logical forms from (possibly graph-structured) UD input.
datasets: German and Spanish translations of WebQuestions
German and Spanish translations of GraphQuestions
baselines: three versions of GRAPHPARSER (entity cliques, dependency trees, CCG-based semantic derivations)
limitations: To achieve language independence, UDEPLAMBDA sacrifices semantic specificity and does not perform lexicalized disambiguation (e.g., distinguishing arg1 vs arg2 for broke with window).
Constructions requiring lexical information—quantifiers (every, some, most), negation markers (no, not), and intentional verbs (believe, said)—are not handled by the current system.
Quantifier handling is discussed but not implemented in the presented system.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multilingual semantic parsing / semantic interface for Universal Dependencies

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics and 8+ years building and evaluating semantic parsing systems and pipelines for research and production. Experienced in experimental design, software engineering for reproducibility, and statistical evaluation of NLP models.
traits: focuses on clarity and completeness of experimental setup (datasets, splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, code availability)
looks for strong, well-justified baselines and ablation studies to support empirical claims
checks whether evaluation metrics, statistical significance, and error analyses properly support conclusions
evaluates implementation details and reproducibility (runtime, dependency on parsers, enhancement heuristics, and failure modes)
expects clear description of training/validation procedures and sensitivity to assumptions (e.g., UD variant differences)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, evidence-driven reviewer who emphasizes rigorous experiments, reproducibility, and clear reporting of methods and results.

reviewer_b.name: Multilingual Semantics Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in formal semantics, cross-lingual NLP, and semantic parsing; has led work on language-independent meaning representations and evaluation of semantic interfaces across languages and resources.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior semantic interfaces and the theoretical contribution to language-independent meaning representation
values discussion of linguistic coverage, limitations across typologically diverse languages, and handling of non-projective or morphologically rich phenomena
looks for careful comparison to previous UD-based and non-UD-based semantic parsers and clarity about what is gained/lost by the proposed approach
concerned with broader impact: resources released, ease of adoption by the community, and potential for extending to more languages or tasks
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware, synthesis-focused reviewer who judges novelty, linguistic adequacy, and potential community impact of the proposed multilingual semantic interface.
